original_abstract: Traditional dependency parsers typically rely on millions of sparse indicator features, which often generalize poorly and suffer from high computational overhead during feature extraction. In this paper, we introduce a novel neural network classifier designed specifically for greedy, transition-based dependency parsing. By learning and utilizing a compact set of dense features, our approach achieves significant gains in both efficiency and accuracy. Our parser demonstrates an improvement of approximately 2% in labeled and unlabeled attachment scores across both English and Chinese datasets. Architecturally, the system is highly optimized, capable of parsing over 1,000 sentences per second while maintaining an unlabeled attachment score of 92.2% on the English Penn Treebank.
humanized_abstract: Traditional dependency parsers rely on millions of sparse indicator features and the features tend to be weak and computationally expensive to extract. In this paper, we propose a new neural network classifier for greedy, transition-based dependency parsing. The parser uses a small set of dense features which lead to a large gain in both speed and accuracy. The experiments are conducted on both the English Wall Street Journal (WSJ) and the Chinese Penn Treebank (CTB) datasets and the gain in LAS and UAS is around 2%. Our parser is very efficient. It parses over 1,000 sentences per second with an UAS of 92.2% on the WSJ dataset.
